author_bio: Author of a single surviving Latin epigram, possibly a later imperial rhetor or poet known only from fragmentary evidence.
author_notes: Identity uncertain; name appears only in late collections.

author_bio: Lucius Accius was a Roman tragic poet and scholar, known for his prolific output and fragments of tragedies and praetextae.
author_notes: Well-attested Republican author.

author_bio: Gaius Aelius Gallus was a Roman jurist and praetor, known for his work on legal language ('De Verbis ad Ius Civile') and as the author of fragments on jurisprudence. He also served as prefect of Egypt under Augustus.
author_notes: Attested in legal sources; fragments preserved by later commentators.
representative_titles: De Verbis ad Ius Civile | iurisprudentia, fragmenta

author_bio: Roman grammarian and scholar, teacher of Varro and Cicero; an important early figure in Roman philology.
author_notes: His original works are lost, but his influence on later scholarship is significant.

author_bio: A Roman historian and antiquarian, known for historical works and a letter to C. Oppius.
author_notes: Fragmentary; partly known through citations and the letter.
representative_titles: Historiae | liber ad C. Oppium, fr.

author_bio: Aemilius Asper was a Latin grammarian and commentator, known for his works on Vergil, Terence, and Sallust. His commentaries survive only in fragments, but he was an important figure in the ancient scholarly tradition.
author_notes: Attested through citations and fragments; exact dates and biography are uncertain.
representative_titles: Vergilius | comment. in Ter. Sall. Verg.

author_display_name: L. Aemilius L. f. M. n. Paulus
author_bio: A Roman general and consul, victor of Pydna (168 BCE). A fragment of an oration survives.
author_notes: Only 62 words of an oration survive.

author_bio: A Roman consul and orator, known only from a very short speech fragment.
author_notes: 15 words of orations survive.

author_bio: Aemilius Macer was a Roman didactic poet, author of lost works on birds, snakes, and plants. Only fragments survive, quoted by later grammarians. He is often associated with the neoteric movement.
author_notes: Fragments known from citations; Ovid mentions him as a friend.

author_bio: Roman statesman and orator; wrote an autobiography and speeches, now fragmentary.
author_notes: Fragments of speeches and autobiography survive only as quotations.

author_bio: A Roman author of a work on chronology, 'De Annis Populi Romani', known only from a citation by Velleius Paterculus.
author_notes: Single fragment; date and identity are speculative.

author_bio: L. Afranius was a Roman playwright of the togata genre (comedy in Roman dress), active in the second century BCE. He was admired for his realistic portrayal of Roman life and his elegance of language. His works survive only in fragments.
author_notes: Well-known figure in Roman literary history; fragments of his togatae comedies are preserved by later authors.

author_bio: Albinovanus Pedo was a Roman poet, known for his epic on the exploits of Germanicus. Only fragments survive, quoted by Seneca the Elder. He belonged to the circle of Ovid.
author_notes: Fragments cited in the elder Seneca and elsewhere.

author_bio: Albinus, possibly a grammarian or historian, authored works on Roman history and metre, preserved only in fragments.
author_notes: Identity unclear; possibly multiple authors.
representative_titles: Rerum Romanarum Liber I | De Metris

author_bio: Publius Alfenus Varus was a Roman jurist, a student of Servius Sulpicius Rufus. His legal writings survive only in fragments, but he is cited in later legal compilations.
author_notes: Attested in legal sources; dates are approximate.

author_bio: Alfius Avitus was a Latin poet, known from a few surviving fragments of his carmina. He is cited by later grammarians, but his life remains obscure.
author_notes: Survives only in fragments; dates and biography are uncertain.

author_display_name: Altercatio Hadriani Augusti et Epicteti Philosophi
author_canonical_name: Altercatio Hadriani Augusti et Epicteti Philosophi
author_bio: The 'Altercatio Hadriani Augusti et Epicteti Philosophi' is a fictional dialogue in Latin between the Emperor Hadrian and the Stoic philosopher Epictetus. It is a literary work of uncertain authorship, likely composed in Late Antiquity for rhetorical or moral instruction.
author_notes: This is a title of a literary work; no actual author is known. The display name functions as the author entry.

author_bio: A Roman educational writer, author of the Liber Memorialis, a short handbook of geography, history, and cosmology, possibly intended for a young audience. Little is known about his life.
author_notes: The name 'Ampelius' appears in the manuscript tradition; identity and date are inferred from internal references.

author_bio: A Stoic philosopher and grammarian, Cornutus was a teacher of Persius and Lucan. His Greek and Latin grammatical works survive only in fragments, but he wrote commentaries and philosophical treatises.
author_notes: His grammatical fragments are cited by later scholiasts; substantial Greek works survive but are not included here.

author_bio: Annianus is a little-known Latin poet, of whom only a few fragments of verse survive.
author_notes: Attribution from late sources; identity unclear.

author_bio: Titus Annius Luscus was a Roman senator and orator of the mid-2nd century BCE. A fragment of a speech (oratio) survives, likely from a political context. He is known from historical sources as a contemporary of Cato the Elder.
author_notes: Fragmentary speech; identity is confirmed by historical testimonia.

author_bio: Label for the corpus of unattributed Latin comic and tragic fragments, including works of unknown authorship from the palliatae, atellanae, togatae, and other dramatic genres.
author_notes: This is a collective designation for anonymous dramatic fragments. The label does not represent a real individual or group.
representative_titles: Tragoediae Poetarum Incertorum | Palliatae Poetarum Incertorum | Atellanae Poetarum Incertorum | Togatae Poetarum Incertorum

author_bio: The 'Anonymi de Differentiis' is a label for an anonymous grammatical treatise on word differences (De Differentiis). The work is of uncertain authorship, likely compiled by an unknown scholar in late antiquity.
author_notes: The heading 'Anonymi de Differentiis' is a modern cataloging label for an anonymous work; no specific author is known.

author_bio: A collective label for numerous anonymous fragments of epic and lyric verse, including carmina popularia, epigrams, and ritual verses, often preserved in antiquarian sources.
author_notes: Label covers many disparate anonymous items; individual provenance often uncertain.
representative_titles: epigrammata et populares versus in Augustum | versus reciproci | versus fortasse Enniani | versus in Caesares Romanos ex Historia Augusta | versus aevi Augustei | carmina Marciana et similia | carmen Saliare | epigramma Pacuvi

author_bio: Anonymous fragments on fiscal law, preserved in a legal compilation.
author_notes: Anonymous text, author unknown.

author_bio: Anonymi Grammatici designates anonymous grammatical fragments of uncertain provenance. These texts represent the work of unknown Latin grammarians, preserved as occasional citations or short treatises.
author_notes: Catch-all heading for anonymous grammatical materials; attribution and date are unknown.

author_bio: Marcus Antistius Labeo was a prominent Roman jurist of the Augustan age, known for his legal writings and his opposition to the jurist Ateius Capito. His works survive only in fragments, but he was highly influential in Roman law.
author_notes: Well-attested in legal sources; dates are approximate but well-established.

author_bio: Marcus Antonius (the orator) was a leading Roman statesman and orator of the late Republic, grandfather of the triumvir. His speeches survive in fragments and were highly praised by Cicero.
author_notes: Distinguished from the triumvir; fragments of speeches preserved.

author_bio: Antonius Panurgus was a Latin grammarian known only from a fragment of a grammatical work preserved in a medieval manuscript. Nothing is known of his life.
author_notes: Extremely obscure; a single grammatical fragment attributed to him.

author_display_name: M. Antonius triumvir r. p. c.
author_bio: Marcus Antonius, the triumvir, was a Roman general and politician, a key figure in the end of the Republic. His speeches, only fragmentarily preserved, reflect his political and oratorical activity.
author_notes: Fragments of orations survive; historical figure of major political importance.

author_bio: A collection of poems transmitted under Vergil's name but of uncertain authorship. Includes works of varying date and quality, likely by multiple poets.
author_notes: Used as a corpus heading for poems possibly by or attributed to Vergil.
representative_titles: Ciris | Culex | Priapea | Copa | Priapeum 'Quid Hoc Novi Est?' | Aetna | Catalepton | Dirae

author_bio: Obscure figure cited for a single fragment; it is unclear whether Aprissius is a person or a textual ghost.
author_notes: The author name is uncertain (marked with '?'); may not refer to a historical person.

author_bio: Apuleius (c. 125–180 CE) was a Roman philosopher and novelist from North Africa, best known for the Metamorphoses (The Golden Ass), as well as rhetorical and philosophical works.
author_notes: Extensive surviving corpus.
representative_titles: Metamorphoses | Apologia | Florida | De Deo Socratis | De Platone et Eius Dogmate | De Mundo | fragmenta | De Deo Socratis, Praef. sp.

author_bio: Aquilius was a Roman comic playwright of the late 2nd to early 1st century BCE, known for writing fabulae palliatae. Only fragments of his work survive, preserving limited evidence of his style.
author_notes: Fragments only; date and identity are uncertain.

author_bio: Gaius Aquilius Gallus was a Roman jurist of the late Republic, a colleague of Cicero and a follower of the Scaevolan legal school. He wrote on civil law and is cited for his opinions on legal formulas, but only tiny fragments of his works survive.
author_notes: Fragmentary only; known through legal sources.

author_bio: Arbonius Silo was a Latin poet, known only from a single surviving fragment of a poem (carmen). His life and works are otherwise unattested.
author_notes: Known only from a brief fragment; biographical details are entirely unknown.

author_display_name: Argumenta Aeneidis et Tetrasticha in cunctis libris Vergilii
author_canonical_name: Argumenta Aeneidis et Tetrasticha in cunctis libris Vergilii
author_bio: This heading refers to a collection of anonymous summaries (argumenta) and tetrasticha (four-line poems) on Vergil's works, including the Aeneid, Bucolics, and Georgics. The works are of uncertain authorship, likely compiled by a late antique scholar.
author_notes: The heading is a modern cataloging label for a set of anonymous works; no specific author is known.
representative_titles: Argumenta Aeneidis, Decasticha | Tetrasticha in Vergilii Bucolica et Georgica | Tetrasticha in Vergilii Aeneida | Argumenta Aeneidis, Monosticha

author_bio: A Roman historian, known for a work on the First Punic War, surviving only in a brief fragment.
author_notes: 41 words survive; date uncertain.

author_bio: Roman jurist active under Domitian and Trajan; fragments of his legal writings are preserved in the Digest.
author_notes: Served as consul and was a notable legal authority. His works survive only in fragments.

author_bio: Q. Asconius Pedianus (fl. 1st century CE) was a Roman grammarian and commentator on Cicero's orations; his commentaries survive partially.
author_notes: Surviving commentaries on five Cicero speeches.
representative_titles: Pro Milone | In Senatu Contra L. Pisonem | Pro Cornelio | Pro Scauro | In Toga Candida

author_bio: Roman senator, orator, and minor poet; son of Asinius Pollio. His works are lost except for fragments.
author_notes: Fragments attributed to him are few and debated.

author_bio: Gaius Asinius Pollio was a Roman politician, orator, historian, and patron of literature; he founded the first public library in Rome.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure.

author_bio: C. Ateius Capito was a prominent Roman jurist of the Augustan and Tiberian periods. A follower of the legal school of A. Cascellius, he wrote extensive works on pontifical law and civil law, cited by later jurists including Justinian's compilers.
author_notes: Well-attested figure; fragments of his legal writings preserved in later digests.

author_bio: Roman grammarian known as a teacher and scholar; fragments of his grammatical works survive.
author_notes: Often called Ateius Praetextatus; his works are cited by later grammarians.

author_bio: Atilius was a Roman comic poet, known only from fragments of his palliatae, likely active in the 2nd century BCE.
author_notes: Extremely fragmentary; almost nothing is known.

author_bio: Roman translator of Homer’s Iliad into Latin; known only from a few fragments and a satirical comment by Persius.
author_notes: Fragments; identity and work known from Persius and later scholia.

author_bio: Aufidius Bassus was a Roman historian, known for his historical works (historiae). He is cited by later authors like Seneca the Elder, but his life and works survive only in fragments.
author_notes: Survives only in fragments; dates and biography are uncertain.

author_bio: Roman jurist, a pupil of Servius Sulpicius Rufus; a fragment of his legal writings survives.
author_notes: Mentioned in Digest sources; his work is known only through quotations.

author_bio: Aufustius was a Roman grammarian of the 1st century BCE, known only through fragments of grammatical and possibly antiquarian works. His contributions to Latin linguistic scholarship are attested by later compilers.
author_notes: Only fragmentary evidence survives; identity and dates are reconstructed from late testimonia.

author_display_name: Augustus Emperor of Rome 63 B.C.-14 A.D.
author_bio: Augustus was the first Roman emperor, a prolific writer whose works include the Res Gestae, letters, edicts, and poems, surviving in fragments.
author_notes: Well-documented historical figure; literary corpus fragmentary but significant.
representative_titles: Res Gestae | opera historica | epistulae | dicta et apophthegmata | edicta | carmina | fragmenta incertae sedis | oratio

author_bio: Aulus Cornelius Celsus was a Roman encyclopedist, best known for his medical treatise De Medicina, which is a major source for ancient medical knowledge. He also wrote on agriculture, rhetoric, and military science, though only the medical work survives substantially.
author_notes: Well-attested historical author; De Medicina is a substantial surviving work, other parts fragmentary.
representative_titles: De Medicina | De Agricultura | De Rhetorica

author_bio: Roman author and grammarian, known for his miscellany Noctes Atticae.
author_notes: Well-attested author.

author_bio: M. Aurelius (121–180 CE) was Roman emperor and Stoic philosopher; a short Latin poem is attributed to him, though its authenticity is uncertain.
author_notes: Attribution to Marcus Aurelius uncertain; possibly spurious.

author_bio: Aurelius Augustinus (Augustine of Hippo) was a Latin Christian bishop, theologian, and philosopher, one of the most influential figures in Western Christianity. The brief Laus Cerei (Praise of the Candle) is a poetic or liturgical piece attributed to him, though its authenticity is debated.
author_notes: The Laus Cerei is a minor work; Augustine's vast corpus is well-attested. Attribution of this piece is uncertain but included in the canon.

author_bio: Late Roman senator and poet; father of the orator Q. Aurelius Symmachus; known from a few fragments of verse.
author_notes: Minimal surviving poetry; information from family relations and later sources.

author_bio: Roman statesman and orator, a leading figure in the generation before Cicero. Elevated to the consulship in 75 BCE. Fragments of his speeches survive.
author_notes: Attested as a historical figure; known mainly through Cicero's references and surviving oratorical fragments.

author_bio: Roman senator and orator, proconsul of Africa; a few of his poems survive, and he was the father-in-law of Boethius.
author_notes: Historical figure; his poetry is minor but distinct.

author_bio: Aurelius Opillus was a Roman grammarian, known from a few fragments of his grammatical work.
author_notes: Very fragmentary; little is known.

author_bio: An obscure technical writer known for a work on forms and enumeration (Expos. et Ratio Omn. Formarum). His identity is unclear; possibly a surveyor or rhetorician. The text survives only in fragments.
author_notes: The author is known only from rare citations. 'Balbus' is a common Roman cognomen, making secure identification impossible.

author_bio: The Bellum Africum is a Latin historical work recounting Julius Caesar's campaign in Africa (47–46 BCE). It is part of the 'Caesarian corpus' of military commentaries, but the author is unknown (likely a subordinate officer). The work is written in a plain, military style.
author_notes: The author is not named; the title functions as the author heading.

author_bio: A Latin historical work, part of the Corpus Caesarianum, detailing the Alexandrian War. The author is unknown, possibly Hirtius or another officer of Caesar.
author_notes: Standard attribution as a work title; authorship debated.

author_bio: An anonymous account of the final campaign of the Caesarian civil war in Spain; preserved as part of the Corpus Caesarianum.
author_notes: Attributed as a work title; author unknown. Part of the Corpus Caesarianum.

author_bio: Bruttedius Niger was a Roman historian whose historical work survives only in fragments.
author_notes: Very fragmentary; known only from a few citations.

author_bio: Two anonymous Latin bucolic poems discovered in a manuscript from Einsiedeln. They imitate Vergil's Eclogues and reflect early Imperial classicism.
author_notes: The name is a modern editorial designation based on provenance.

author_bio: A Roman noble, perhaps the Caecilius Metellus who was consul in 251 BCE, known for a single verse insult against the poet Naevius. The fragment is one of the earliest examples of Roman literary polemic.
author_notes: Identity uncertain; the fragment is cited by later grammarians without clear context.

author_bio: Quintus Caecilius Metellus Macedonicus was a Roman statesman, general, and orator. He served as consul in 143 BCE and was known for his military campaigns in Macedonia. A fragment of a speech (oratio) survives.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; dates are well-established.

author_bio: Roman general and statesman, victor in the Jugurthine War. Known as an orator, with fragments of his speeches preserved.
author_notes: Fragments of his speeches survive.

author_bio: Roman comic poet, a major figure of the palliata tradition; fragments of his plays are preserved.
author_notes: Considered by some ancient critics as the greatest comic playwright after Plautus and Terence.

author_bio: A Roman Etruscan nobleman and writer, possibly of historical or antiquarian works, known only from a fragment.
author_notes: Single fragment of 51 words; identity uncertain.

author_bio: Name associated with the sole surviving Roman cookbook, 'De Re Coquinaria'. The work is a compilation from various sources, possibly not by a single author.
author_notes: The attribution is traditional and may be pseudonymous; the text is a composite.
representative_titles: De Re Coquinaria | Brevis Ciborum, excerpta | Brevis Pimentorum, excerpta

author_bio: Roman medical writer, translator of the Greek physician Soranus, known for his works on acute and chronic diseases.
author_notes: Historical author; his medical compilations are partly extant.

author_bio: Marcus Caelius Rufus was a Roman orator and politician, a correspondent of Cicero, and known for his turbulent career and speeches. Only fragments of his orations survive.
author_notes: Known through Cicero's letters and legal sources.

author_bio: Caesellius Vindex was a Roman grammarian; only fragments of his grammatical works survive.
author_notes: Fragments cited by later grammarians.

author_bio: A Roman grammarian and poet, author of metrical treatises and possibly lyric verse. His works on meters influenced later Latin grammarians.
author_notes: Fragments only; some works attributed to him are of uncertain authorship.
representative_titles: De Compositionibus sp. | De Metris, fragmenta | Breviatio Pedum sp. | De Metris Horatii sp. | carmen | Genera Versuum sp. | Poeticae Species Lat. sp.

author_bio: Sulpicia, the wife of Calenus, is a rare instance of a female Roman poet. Two works are attributed: a political poem (De Statu Rei Publicae, of uncertain authenticity) and a personal carmen. She is known from references in the literary tradition.
author_notes: The attribution is based on ancient citations, but some scholars doubt the authenticity of the political work. 'Caleni uxor' is a descriptive heading.
representative_titles: De Statu Rei Publicae sp. | carmen

author_bio: Roman orator mentioned by Cicero as a distinguished speaker. Only fragments of his speeches survive.
author_notes: Fragments preserved by Cicero and others.

author_bio: Latin declaimer whose excerpted declamations survive from the rhetorical schools.
author_notes: Survives only in excerpt; identity and dates uncertain.

author_bio: Gaius Calpurnius Piso was a Roman senator and orator of the late Republic, known for an oratio preserved only in fragments. He served as consul in 58 BCE and was a prominent figure in the political struggles of the period.
author_notes: Fragmentary oration; historical context well-attested.

author_bio: L. Calpurnius Piso Censorinus Frugi was a Roman annalist and censor, author of a lost historical work (Annales) covering Roman history from early times to his own day. Fragments are preserved in later grammarians and historians.
author_notes: Historical figure; his Annales survive only in fragments cited by later authors.

author_bio: A Latin bucolic poet, author of seven Eclogae, imitating Virgil and Theocritus, and possibly associated with the court of Nero. His identity remains debated.
author_notes: Attribution to 'Calpurnius Siculus' is traditional but not secure; dating is contested.

author_bio: Roman orator mentioned by Cicero; only a single fragment of a speech survives.
author_notes: Known only from a quotation in Cicero's Brutus.

author_bio: Canon is a title of a compilation or collection, likely of legal or scriptural content, occupying the author slot in the corpus.
author_notes: Ambiguous label; may refer to a collection of texts rather than an individual author.

author_bio: An ancient hymn preserved in inscriptions of the Arval Brethren, representing one of the oldest known Latin religious texts.
author_notes: The Carmen Arvale is a textual artifact, not an author. It is assigned here as a work-title occupying the author field.

author_bio: A Latin poem on the Alexandrian War, possibly written in the 1st century AD, surviving in fragments.
author_notes: The work is a fragmentary poem; author unknown.

author_bio: A short Latin ritual prayer or carmen for ritual devotion (devotio), preserved as a textual artifact.
author_notes: Anonymous ritual text; date and context are uncertain.

author_bio: The 'Carmen Evocationis' is a Latin ritual hymn for summoning (evocatio) a tutelary deity during military siege. It is preserved in the ancient liturgical tradition and attributed to no known author, functioning as a textual artifact rather than a personal composition.
author_notes: This is a title of a ritual text (carmen) used in Roman religio-military practice; the 'author' slot is occupied by the work title itself, not a person.

author_bio: Roman author of a lost work 'Liber Bene Dictorum', from which only two fragments survive, quoted by Macrobius.
author_notes: Possibly related to the jurist Cascellius; uncertain identity.

author_bio: Cassius Hemina was a Roman historian, author of annales covering early Roman history; only fragments remain.
author_notes: Very fragmentary; known from citations.

author_bio: Roman orator and jurist, possibly the tribune of 104 BCE. A fragment of a speech survives.
author_notes: Historical figure; identification with known Cassii uncertain.

author_bio: One of the greatest Roman lyric and epigrammatic poets; central figure in the neoteric movement.
author_notes: Well-attested; a cornerstone of Latin literary study.

author_cultural_context: Latin translation and commentary of Plato
author_bio: Chalcidius was a Latin translator and commentator of Plato's Timaeus, whose work was influential in the Middle Ages.
author_notes: Well-known figure in the transmission of Platonic thought.

author_bio: Lucius Cincius was a Roman jurist and grammarian, known for his works on law (iurisprudentia) and grammar (grammatica). He is cited by later authors, but his life and works survive only in fragments.
author_notes: Survives only in fragments; dates and biography are inferred from citations.

author_bio: Roman senator, annalist, and antiquarian known for a history of Rome written in Greek. A grammatical fragment attributed to him may derive from a later homonym.
author_notes: The grammatical fragment is possibly spurious or misattributed.

author_bio: A Roman censor, consul, and statesman remembered for building the Appian Way and the first aqueduct. He is also credited with sententiae and early oratorical works, making him a key figure in early Latin prose.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; the surviving fragments are attributed by later Roman authors.

author_bio: Roman general and member of the Julio-Claudian dynasty, known for his campaigns in Germany and his Latin adaptation of Aratus' 'Phaenomena'.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; his translation of Aratus is partially extant.

author_bio: Roman annalist whose 'Annales' covered early Roman history in a simple style, influencing the later historical tradition.
author_notes: Historical author; his work survives only in fragments cited by later grammarians and historians.

author_bio: A Roman grammarian known only from a few grammatical fragments.
author_notes: Very obscure; little information available.

author_bio: Roman grammarian and teacher; a fragment of his grammatical work is attributed to him.
author_notes: Distinct from the later Servius (commentator on Vergil). His work is known from scanty quotations.

author_bio: Roman historian, wrote a history of Rome.
author_notes: Only a single fragment survives.

author_bio: Roman politician, notorious for the Bona Dea scandal. He was also an orator; fragments of his speeches survive.
author_notes: Fragments collected by Malcovati.

author_bio: Roman grammarian known only from a meager fragment preserved by Charisius.
author_notes: Extremely fragmentary; nothing known of his life.

author_bio: Roman annalist, wrote a history of the Second Punic War (the first Roman historical monograph). Fragments survive.
author_notes: Fragments in Peter, HRR.

author_bio: P. Cominius was a Roman orator known only from a fragmentary speech cited by Quintilian. Nothing else is known of his life or career.
author_notes: Known solely from a citation in Quintilian's Institutio Oratoria; no independent biographical information.

author_bio: A collection of augural records and commentary from Roman religious practice.
author_notes: Only fragments survive.

author_bio: The Commentarii Consulares were official records of Roman consuls; only fragmentary quotations survive.
author_notes: Quotation fragments from an official record.

author_display_name: Commentarius Anquisitionis M'. Sergii M'. f. Quaestoris
author_canonical_name: Commentarius Anquisitionis M'. Sergii M'. f. Quaestoris
author_bio: A procedural commentary from a Roman quaestor (Manius Sergius), preserved only as a fragment.
author_notes: The title itself is the author entry; it is a textual work, not a person.

author_bio: Publius Cornelius Dolabella was a Roman politician and orator of the late Republic, known for a speech (oratio) preserved only in a tiny fragment. He was a partisan of Julius Caesar and later served as consul suffectus in 44 BCE.
author_notes: Fragmentary oration; biographical details known from historical sources.

author_bio: Roman freedman of Sulla, who wrote on grammatical topics; one surviving fragment.
author_notes: Fragment from a grammatical work; known via Suetonius and Charisius.

author_bio: M. Cornelius Fronto (c. 100–170 CE) was a Roman orator and teacher of rhetoric, known from his surviving correspondence and treatises.
author_notes: Extensive corpus of letters.
representative_titles: De Eloquentia | Ad Verum Imp. Epistulae | De Feriis Alsiensibus | Ad M. Caesarem et Invicem | Ad M. Antoninum Imp. Epist. | Ad Antoninum Pium Epistulae | De Nepote Amisso | Ad Amicos Epistulae

author_bio: Roman poet, orator, and first prefect of Egypt. A pioneer of Latin love elegy, his poetry was largely lost until the discovery of a papyrus fragment at Qasr Ibrim.
author_notes: The papyrus fragment preserves portions of his elegies.
representative_titles: elegia in pap. Qas?r Ibrîm | elegia

author_bio: Roman senator and poet, consul in 26 CE. Known for a few poetic fragments and his involvement in political conspiracies.
author_notes: Historical figure; his poetry is known only from brief citations.

author_bio: A Roman senator and orator, known from a few fragments of speeches.
author_notes: Only 64 words of orations survive.

author_bio: Cornelius Nepos was a Roman biographer and historian, best known for his collection of biographies De Viris Illustribus. His work is a key source for the lives of famous Greek and Roman figures and reflects the intellectual milieu of the late Republic.
author_notes: Well-attested historical author; substantial surviving corpus including the 'Vitae' and fragments.

author_display_name: P. Cornelius Scipio Aemilianus Africanus minor
author_canonical_name: Scipio Aemilianus Africanus minor, P. Cornelius
author_bio: Roman general and statesman, destroyer of Carthage; known for his oratory, of which fragments survive.
author_notes: Major historical figure; oratorical fragments known from ancient sources.

author_bio: Roman general and statesman, celebrated for defeating Hannibal at Zama; known also for a preserved speech fragment.
author_notes: Major historical figure; only a few oratorical fragments survive.

author_bio: A Roman senator and orator, known for a fragmentary speech.
author_notes: Historical figure, but only fragments remain.

author_bio: Roman epic poet, known from fragments of a historical poem (perhaps on the Sicilian War or the civil wars).
author_notes: Fragments collected by Morel; some attributions uncertain.

author_bio: Roman historian and novelist, known for his Histories and Milesian tales.
author_notes: Fragments survive.

author_bio: Lucius Cornelius Sulla was a Roman general and dictator, known for his memoirs ('Commentarii Rerum Gestarum'), which survive in fragments and served as a source for later historians. His political reforms reshaped the late Republic.
author_notes: Fragments of memoirs; Sulla's historical importance is paramount.

author_bio: Preeminent Roman historian and senator, author of the 'Annales' and 'Historiae', as well as the 'Germania', 'Agricola', and 'Dialogus de Oratoribus'. Acclaimed for his concise, often critical style.
author_notes: Well-attested historical author; his major historical works are largely extant.
representative_titles: Historiae | Annales | De Origine et Situ Germanorum | De Vita Iulii Agricolae | Dialogus de Oratoribus

author_bio: Roman poet and orator, a friend of Cicero; only fragments of his poems survive.
author_notes: Mentioned by Cicero and others. His poetic fragments are cited by later grammarians.

author_bio: Cornificius Gallus is a shadowy figure; only a single verse fragment in response to Vergil survives.
author_notes: Attribution uncertain; possibly from a lost poet.

author_bio: Obscure Roman grammarian cited by later authors for his work on grammar.
author_notes: Very fragmentary; little known.

author_bio: Roman historian, author of a history of the civil wars and the Augustan period. His work was suppressed under Tiberius; only fragments survive.
author_notes: Fragments in Peter, HRR.

author_bio: A compilation of didascaliae and argumenta (production notes and summaries) for the comedies of Plautus, likely assembled by a later grammarian.
author_notes: The work is a compilation; individual authorship unknown.
representative_titles: Aulularia | Menaechmi | Trinummus | Curculio | Miles Gloriosus | Pseudolus | Mercator | Amphitruo

author_bio: The 'Didascaliae et Periochae' are ancient scholarly summaries and introductory notes to the comedies of Terence, providing production details and plot summaries. They are transmitted in manuscripts as metatexts.
author_notes: Treats a collective set of scholia; the author slot is occupied by the textual group.
representative_titles: Andria | Heauton Timorumenos | Eunuchus | Phormio | Adelphoe | Hecyra

author_bio: A Roman politician and orator, consul in 96 BCE, known for a speech of which a tiny fragment survives.
author_notes: Only 12 words of one oration survive.

author_bio: Roman poet, known for his epigrams and elegies.
author_notes: Only fragments survive.

author_bio: Roman poet known only from a single fragment; period and identity are uncertain.
author_notes: The name is rare and the attribution rests on a single quotation. Possibly a late Republican or Imperial figure.

author_bio: A Roman orator of the late Republic, known only from a single fragment of a speech preserved by later grammarians.
author_notes: Fragmentary speech; otherwise unrecorded.

author_bio: Egnatius is a little-known Roman author of a single hexameter work on natural philosophy, 'De Rerum Natura,' of which only a fragment survives. His identity is obscure but likely dates to the late Republic.
author_notes: Only a single fragment survives; the name is attested but minimal.

author_bio: The English Bible in the King James Version (Authorized Version) is a 1611 translation of the Christian scriptures into English, commissioned by King James I. It had profound influence on English language and literature.
author_notes: This entry represents the translated text as a work occupying the author slot, not an individual translator.
representative_titles: Lamentations | 1 Samuel | 2 Timothy | Genesis | Acts | John | Romans | 1 Corinthians

author_bio: The Revised Standard Version (RSV) of the English Bible, a scholarly translation of the Christian scriptures and apocrypha. Cataloged here as a work title because the author heading is the title of the textual corpus, not a person.
author_notes: The author display name refers to a translation of the Bible, not an individual or group author. Hence author_agent_kind is work_title.
representative_titles: Joshua | Job | Baruch | Nahum | Luke | John | Galatians | 1 Peter

author_bio: Quintus Ennius, often called the father of Roman poetry, wrote the epic Annales and many tragedies, influencing later Latin literature.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; works survive in fragments.
representative_titles: Annales | tragoediae | Saturae | varia | incerta | palliatae | praetextae

author_bio: Sextus (or possibly Spurius) Ennius was a Roman grammarian of uncertain date, known only through fragments of grammatical writings. The name is sometimes confused with the poet Ennius, but he is a distinct figure in the Latin grammatical tradition.
author_notes: Name and remains are extremely uncertain; the praenomen is disputed.

author_bio: C. Erucius was a Roman orator known from a fragment of a speech cited by Quintilian. No further biographical details survive.
author_notes: Known only from a single citation in Quintilian's Institutio Oratoria.

author_bio: The Caepasii brothers (Gaius and Lucius) were Roman orators, known from a single fragment of a speech (oratio). They are mentioned by Cicero, but their individual lives are obscure.
author_notes: Known only from a single citation; the heading represents a collective attribution.

author_bio: Fabius Dossennus is an obscure Roman poet of the early Republican period, known from a single dramatic fragment. His name suggests he may have been a writer of comedy or satire.
author_notes: Only one fragment survives; the name is very poorly attested.

author_bio: Roman politician and annalist, consul in 139 BCE. Fragments of his 'Annales' survive.
author_notes: Historical figure; his historical work survives only in fragments.

author_bio: Fabius Pictor was one of the earliest Roman historians, writing in Greek and Latin; fragments of his Annales and legal works survive.
author_notes: Often cited by later historians; fragments.

author_bio: Roman historian and orator, a contemporary of the Gracchi. He wrote historical works and delivered speeches, fragments of which survive.
author_notes: Often identified with the consul of 122 BCE, but uncertain.

author_bio: Greek sophist and philosopher who wrote in both Greek and Latin. A eunuch by birth, he was a prominent intellectual figure in Hadrianic Rome. Only fragments of his Latin works survive.
author_notes: The Latin oration fragment is attributed to him but context is unclear.

author_bio: A Roman historian and antiquarian known for his annalistic work, Fenestella was active in the late Republic. His Annales covered early Roman history, but only fragments survive, cited by later grammarians and historians.
author_notes: Fenestella is a fragmentary author; his identity and dating depend on rare citations, making secure biography difficult.

author_bio: Roman grammarian, author of works on orthography and doubtful words.
author_notes: Fragments survive.

author_bio: The Fragmenta Bobiensia is a modern collection of grammatical fragments from the Bobbio monastery, attributed to various ancient authors.
author_notes: Not a single author; a compilation of fragments from different sources.
representative_titles: De Structuris | De Finalibus Syllabis | De Versibus | De Accentibus | De Littera | De Nomine | De Metris | De Propriis Nominibus

author_bio: Early Roman poet, known from a few fragments of verse (annalistic epic).
author_notes: Fragments preserved by later grammarians.

author_bio: A neoteric poet of the late Republic, known for satirical and epigrammatic verse. His works survive only in fragments.
author_notes: Small surviving corpus; biographical details are uncertain.

author_bio: Gaius was a Roman jurist, author of the Institutes, a foundational legal textbook that survived largely intact.
author_notes: Well-attested; his Institutes are a key source for Roman law.
representative_titles: Institutiones | Gai Institutionum epitome | Institut., frr. Aeg. et Oxyrh.

author_bio: Gaius Valerius Flaccus was a Roman poet, author of the epic 'Argonautica', which recounts the story of Jason and the Argonauts. He likely died before completing the work. His life is poorly documented.
author_notes: Attested through his work and a few external references; dates are approximate.

author_bio: Gallus Antipater was a Roman historian, known only from fragmentary references to his historical works (historiae). He is cited by later authors, but his life and career remain obscure.
author_notes: Survives only in fragments; identity and dates are inferred from citations.

author_bio: A Roman poet known only from a few fragments of verse.
author_notes: Extremely fragmentary; nothing known about life.

author_bio: Roman grammarian and antiquarian, known for his work 'De Origine Vocabulorum', fragments of which survive.
author_notes: Mentioned by later scholars such as Aulus Gellius; his etymological work is fragmentary.

author_bio: Gnaeus Gellius, a Roman annalist whose historical work survives only in fragments.
author_notes: Fragmentary historian.

author_bio: A shadowy figure known from citations of tragic fragments, possibly a noble amateur tragedian. The name suggests affiliation with the Gracchi family, but the dramatic works cannot be securely attributed.
author_notes: The author is known only via a few tragic fragments; identity and period are highly uncertain. May be a confusion with another Gracchus.

author_bio: Granius Flaccus was a Roman jurist, known from fragments of his work on civil law. He is cited in later legal compilations as an authority on procedural rules.
author_notes: Known from legal fragments; minimal biographical data.

author_bio: Granius Licinianus was a Roman historian whose annalistic work survives only in fragments, covering early Roman history.
author_notes: Fragmentary author known from a single manuscript.

author_bio: Roman poet, author of the didactic poem 'Cynegetica' on hunting with dogs, a work in the tradition of Virgil's Georgics.
author_notes: Little known about his life; his poem survives incomplete.

author_period: 1st century CE (original), Latin translations from late antiquity
author_bio: The Greek New Testament in its standard edition, here represented in Latin translation.
author_notes: Textual corpus, not a person.
representative_titles: Matthew | Acts | Mark | 1 Corinthians | Romans | 1 John | Galatians | Philemon

author_bio: The Roman emperor Hadrian was also a poet and orator. His carmina and orationes survive only in fragments, but he was renowned for his learning and patronage of arts. He is one of the few Roman emperors with a literary corpus.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; fragments are preserved by later authors and inscriptions.

author_bio: The Hebrew Bible (Masoretic Text / Biblia Hebraica Stuttgartensia) is the canonical collection of Jewish scriptures. In Latin contexts it is cited as a source for the Vulgate and Latin patristic literature.
author_notes: Cited as a textual corpus; used as an author heading for biblical quotations in Latin texts.
representative_titles: Zechariah (Zachariah) | Psalms | Deuteronomy | 1 Samuel | Proverbs | Genesis | Ezekiel | 1 Kings

author_bio: A neoteric poet and friend of Catullus, known for highly learned and mythological poetry. Only a few fragments survive.
author_notes: Fragments only; his identity is sometimes confused with the tribune Cinna.

author_bio: Roman orator and freedman known for his sharp wit. Fragments of his speeches are cited by Roman authors.
author_notes: Known mainly through references in Cicero and others.

author_bio: A Roman orator, known only from a brief fragment of a speech.
author_notes: Only 62 words survive; life is otherwise unknown.

author_bio: Bishop of Arles and poet; known for his sermons and a few poems. His literary output survives in fragmentary form.
author_notes: Fragments of his poems are collected in various anthologies.

author_bio: Aulus Hirtius was a Roman politician, military commander, and historian. He is best known for writing the eighth book of Caesar's 'De Bello Gallico' and for his letters. He served as consul in 43 BCE and died in battle.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; dates are well-established.
representative_titles: De Bello Gallico Liber VIII | epistulae

author_cultural_context: Latin literature, late antique epic (Ilias Latina)
author_bio: Homerus Latinus refers to the epitomist of the Ilias Latina, a 1st-century CE Latin abridgment of Homer's Iliad in hexameter verse. The work is a school text and likely by an anonymous grammarian rather than a single known author.
author_notes: Traditional attribution as a pseudonymous or conventional name for the epitomist; the actual author's identity is unknown.

author_cultural_context: Latin poetry (Carmina, Ars Poetica, Epistulae, Epodi, Carmen Saeculare, Sermones)
author_bio: Quintus Horatius Flaccus (Horace) was the leading Augustan poet, renowned for his Odes (Carmina), Satires (Sermones), Epistles (Epistulae), and the Ars Poetica. His consummate lyricism, urbane wit, and moral wisdom have made him a central figure in Western literature.
author_notes: Well-preserved corpus with ample biographical documentation.
representative_titles: Carmina | Ars Poetica | Epistulae | Epodi | Carmen Saeculare | Sermones

author_bio: Quintus Hortensius Hortalus was a renowned Roman orator, rival of Cicero, and a statesman. He also wrote poetry, but his oratorical works survive only in fragments. His style was considered more Asianist than Ciceronian.
author_notes: Fragments of speeches and poems preserved by Cicero and later authors.

author_bio: Hostius was a Roman poet who wrote an epic on the Histrian War; only fragments survive.
author_notes: Fragments of Bellum Histricum.

author_bio: Hyginus is the traditional name of the author of the Fabulae, a Latin compendium of mythology; his identity and date are uncertain.
author_notes: Author of Fabulae; attribution traditional.

author_bio: Author of the 'De Astronomia', a handbook on mythology and astronomy. The work is a compilation; the name Hyginus may be a pseudonym or a misattribution.
author_notes: Distinct from C. Iulius Hyginus; the Astronomica is likely a later epitome.

author_bio: Roman land surveyor and technical writer, author of several treatises on land surveying (gromatics) and boundary disputes.
author_notes: Historical figure; distinct from the mythographer Hyginus.
representative_titles: De Generibus Controversiarum | Constitutio Limitum sp. | De Munition. Castrorum sp. | De Condicionibus Agrorum | De Limitibus

author_bio: Trajan was Roman emperor, known for his Dacian wars and a lost work Dacica, of which only a tiny fragment survives.
author_notes: Fragmentary; only one word survives from Dacica.

author_bio: Nero was the fifth Roman emperor, also known for his poetic compositions, of which only a few fragments survive.
author_notes: Fragmentary poetic output; historical figure widely attested.

author_bio: Roman orator active in the imperial period, known only through a single surviving speech. Likely a different figure from the later Christian writer Sextus Iulius Africanus.
author_notes: Very little biographical information survives; fragments indicate an orator.

author_bio: A little-known author, possibly of historical works, from whom a single fragment survives.
author_notes: Very sparse evidence; only 62 words. Identity and date uncertain.

author_bio: L. Iulius Caesar (fl. early 1st century BCE) was a Roman politician and author of a work on auspices, fragments of which survive.
author_notes: Only fragments remain; attribution secure.

author_bio: C. Iulius Caesar Strabo was a Roman orator, politician, and tragic poet. He was a member of the patrician Julian family and held the office of aedile. His orations and tragedies survive only in fragments, yet he is noted for his role in the literary culture of the late Republic.
author_notes: Historical figure; fragments from his speeches and tragedies preserved by Cicero and others.

author_bio: A Roman senator, governor, and military writer, Frontinus served as curator aquarum and wrote De Aquis Urbis Romae, a technical treatise on Rome's water supply. His Strategemata is a collection of military stratagems, and he also wrote on land surveying.
author_notes: Biography well-attested; all works transmitted intact.
representative_titles: De Aquis Urbis Romae | Strategemata | De Arte Mensoria | De Agrorum Qualitate | De Controversiis | De Limitibus

author_bio: Roman scholar and writer, a freedman of Augustus. He wrote on grammar, history, and geography; most works lost.
author_notes: Often conflated with Hyginus Astronomus; distinct persons.

author_bio: Iulius Modestus was a Roman grammarian, presumably active in the first century CE. His grammatical works survive only in fragments cited by later grammarians such as Priscian.
author_notes: Fragmentary grammatical citations; he is known from references in later grammarians.

author_bio: Roman poet, known from a few fragments; possibly the friend of Seneca and Lucan mentioned in ancient sources.
author_notes: Fragments in Morel and Courtney.

author_bio: Iulius Valerius was a Latin author known for a poetic work (carmina), likely active in the late antique period. His identity is attested in manuscript traditions, though details of his life remain sparse.
author_notes: Attested only through a single surviving work; biographical details are uncertain.

author_bio: Roman jurist (iurisconsultus) known for a single fragment of legal writing.
author_notes: Fragmentary legal text; identity uncertain among the Bruti.

author_bio: Roman senator and one of the assassins of Julius Caesar. Known also as an orator and writer, though only fragments of his speeches survive.
author_notes: The abbreviation 'tyr.' (tyrannicida) references his role in Caesar's assassination.

author_bio: A Roman jurist and antiquarian who wrote commentarii on constitutional and legal history. His work survives only in citations, notably on the history of the Roman tribes and magistracies.
author_notes: Attested by Roman legal sources; the cognomen 'Gracchanus' suggests a connection to Gaius Gracchus, but his works are fragmentary.

author_bio: Roman author who translated Mago's Carthaginian agricultural manual into Latin. Only a few fragments survive.
author_notes: Fragments in the De Agri Cultura tradition.

author_bio: Justinian (Flavius Petrus Sabbatius Iustinianus) was Byzantine emperor from 527 to 565 CE. He commissioned the codification of Roman law, notably the Digesta (Pandectae), which compiled juristic writings and became a foundational text of civil law.
author_notes: Comprehensive and well-attested; the Digesta are a massive compilation from earlier jurists.

author_bio: Roman comic poet of the palliata genre; only a few fragments of his plays survive.
author_notes: Very little is known about his life and work; fragments cited by later grammarians.

author_bio: Roman senator and orator known from Cicero's correspondence and a fragment of a speech.
author_notes: Historical figure; very few fragments survive.

author_bio: John Milton (1608–1674) was an English poet and intellectual, best known for his epic poem Paradise Lost and for a body of Latin prose and verse that engaged with classical and contemporary political themes.
author_notes: English and Latin author; Latin works include Defensio pro Populo Anglicano.
representative_titles: Paradise Lost (English) | Defensionem Regiam (Latin Works, vol. 7, pp. 1-300)

author_bio: Gaius Iulius Caesar was a Roman general, statesman, and author. His commentaries on the Gallic and Civil Wars are masterpieces of Latin prose and remain primary sources for the history of the late Republic. He also produced works on grammar, poetry, and speeches, many of which are lost.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure with extensive surviving (and fragmentary) writings.
representative_titles: De Bello Gallico | Bellum Civile | epistulae ad familiares | epistulae ad Ciceronem | Anticato | orationes | carmina | De Analogia

author_bio: Decimus Iunius Iuvenalis (Juvenal) was the foremost Roman satirist of the Imperial period, active in the late 1st and early 2nd centuries CE. His sixteen hexameter satires excoriate the vices and follies of Roman society, with a caustic and rhetorical style that has influenced Western satire profoundly.
author_notes: Well-preserved corpus; biographical details are limited but generally accepted.

author_bio: Laberius was a Roman knight and writer of mimes, forced by Caesar to perform on stage; fragments of his mimes survive.
author_notes: Fragmentary; important for the history of Roman theater.

author_bio: Titus Labienus was a Roman orator and historian, known for his outspoken Republican sympathies. His works were ordered destroyed by the Senate, but fragments survive in quotations. He was nicknamed 'Rabienus' (the Mad) for his vehemence.
author_notes: Survival minimal; known primarily through Seneca the Elder.

author_bio: A Roman jurist known from surviving fragments of his legal writings, particularly on civil law. He is cited by later legal compilations such as the Digest.
author_notes: Only fragments preserved in later sources; no independent biography.

author_bio: Roman statesman and orator, friend of Scipio Aemilianus and a central figure in the Scipionic Circle; fragments of his speeches survive.
author_notes: Cicero portrays him as a wise man; his historical oratory is preserved in fragments.

author_bio: Laevius was a Roman poet of the late Republic; fragments of his poems, including some dubia, survive.
author_notes: Fragments.
representative_titles: carmina | fr. dubium a Morel omissum

author_bio: The Latin translation of the Bible primarily attributed to Jerome, known as the Vulgate.
author_notes: Complex textual history.
representative_titles: Genesis | Psalms (from the Old Greek) | Psalms (from the Hebrew) | Isaiah | Luke | 1 Corinthians | Romans | Judith

author_bio: A hexameter panegyric in praise of a Piso, the Laus Pisonis is transmitted anonymously and is often attributed to a young Calpurnius Siculus or an unknown poet. The work is a valuable example of imperial encomium.
author_notes: The author is the text itself; no personal author is named in the manuscript tradition. The title occupies the author slot.

author_bio: Author of a mime, known only from a single fragment. Possibly a member of the Cornelii Lentuli family.
author_notes: Identity is uncertain; the fragment is the sole attestation.

author_bio: Lucius Licinius Crassus was a celebrated Roman orator and statesman, a leading figure in the generation before Cicero. His speeches, known from fragments, were praised for their elegance and force.
author_notes: Fragments preserved by Cicero and others.

author_bio: Minor Roman playwright, possible author of palliatae (comedy in Greek dress). Known only from a single fragment.
author_notes: The name appears in a fragment list; virtually nothing is otherwise known.

author_bio: C. Licinius Macer was a Roman annalist and orator, known for his historical work (Annales) and speeches. He was also a praetor and a supporter of the popular party, and his son, Licinius Macer Calvus, was a noted poet and orator.
author_notes: Historical figure with surviving fragments from his orations and annalistic history.

author_bio: Calvus was a Roman poet and orator, a member of the neoterics, known for his love poems and speeches; only fragments remain.
author_notes: Fragmentary corpus; important for literary history.

author_bio: Roman senator and historian, known from fragments.
author_notes: Only a few fragments survive.

author_bio: A Greek freedman from Tarentum, regarded as the founder of Latin literature; he translated the Odyssey into Saturnian verse and wrote the first Latin tragedies and comedies.
author_notes: Fragmentary corpus; pioneering role firmly attested.

author_bio: Lucan was a Roman poet and a leading figure of the Silver Age, best known for his epic poem 'Bellum Civile' (Pharsalia), which chronicles the civil war between Caesar and Pompey. His work is noted for its rhetorical intensity and Stoic themes.
author_notes: Major epic poet; works include a lost poem on the fall of Troy.

author_bio: The pioneer of Roman satire, whose thirty books of Saturae influenced Horace, Persius, and Juvenal. Only fragments survive, but attest his wide-ranging social criticism.
author_notes: Life well-attested; work lost except fragments.

author_bio: Lucilius Iunior is a Latin poet known from a small collection of poems (carmina) attributed to him in the Latin Anthology. He is sometimes identified with the Lucilius who was a friend of Seneca the Younger, but the identification is uncertain.
author_notes: Fragmentary poetic corpus; the name distinguishes him from the satirist Gaius Lucilius, but evidence is thin.

author_bio: Florus was a Roman historian and poet, author of an epitome of Roman history and some poems in the Anthologia Latina.
author_notes: Well-preserved; important for Roman historical tradition.
representative_titles: Epitome Bell. Omn. Ann. DCC | carmina in Anthologia Latina | Vergilius Orator an Poeta | carmina | epist. ad imperat. Hadrianum

author_bio: Lucius Annaeus Seneca (Seneca the Younger) was a Roman Stoic philosopher, statesman, and playwright. He authored numerous works including 'Epistulae Morales ad Lucilium', tragedies like 'Thyestes' and 'Agamemnon', and philosophical dialogues. He served as advisor to Emperor Nero and was forced to commit suicide.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; extensive biographical and textual tradition.
representative_titles: Epistulae Morales ad Lucilium | Thyestes | Agamemnon | De Clementia | Troades | Oedipus | Dialogi | Hercules Oetaeus

author_bio: Columella was a Roman writer on agriculture, whose comprehensive work De Re Rustica is an important source on Roman farming.
author_notes: Well-preserved corpus.

author_bio: Roman poet and philosopher, author of 'De Rerum Natura', a didactic epic expounding Epicurean physics and ethics in splendid Latin verse.
author_notes: Well-attested historical author; his major poem survives.

author_bio: Luscius Lanuvinus was a Roman comic playwright of the early 2nd century BCE, active alongside or slightly before Terence. He wrote fabulae palliatae but only tiny fragments survive. He is known for his stylistic conservatism and rivalry with Terence.
author_notes: Fragmentary remains; biographical details are inferred from Terence's prologues.

author_bio: Roman politician and patron of letters, notably remembered for his epigrams and historical writings, representative of early Roman literary culture.
author_notes: Attested as a figure active in late 2nd to early 1st century BCE. The historical record is fragmentary.

author_bio: Roman orator and son of the elder Catulus; a fragment of his oratory is preserved, though his career is largely overshadowed by his father.
author_notes: Distinguished from his father by the 'iunior' epithet. Very few textual remains.

author_bio: Manius Manilius was a Roman jurist of the late 2nd or early 1st century BCE, a member of the legal Scaevolan school. His works on civil law are cited by later authorities, though only fragments survive.
author_notes: Fragmentary; known through legal compilations.

author_bio: Gaius Maecenas, a wealthy Roman patron of letters and trusted advisor to Augustus, supported poets such as Horace and Virgil. His own poetic fragments are slight but reflect his engagement with contemporary literary trends.
author_notes: Known primarily as a patron; his poetic output is fragmentary.

author_bio: A Roman poet and astrologer, author of the didactic Astronomica in five books, the earliest complete Latin work on astrology. His identity is otherwise unknown, but his poem shows Stoic and Hellenistic influence.
author_notes: Although the poet is securely named, little is known of his life; the work survives complete.

author_bio: Roman poet known for his didactic work on astronomy and astrology, the 'Astronomica'. Likely active during the reigns of Augustus and Tiberius.
author_notes: Manilius is a historical figure, though biographical details are scant. His 'Astronomica' survives in part.

author_bio: L. Marcius Philippus was a Roman orator and politician, known for his eloquence and his role in the civil wars. His speeches are lost except for a few fragments cited by later authors.
author_notes: Historical orator; fragments survive in rhetorical works.

author_bio: A shadowy early Roman prophet (vates) and poet, said to have composed oracular or didactic praecepta. He is known only from citations by later writers, and his historicity is uncertain.
author_notes: Marcius is a legendary figure; his works are anonymous and may be pseudepigraphic. The cognomen 'vates' indicates a prophetic role.

author_bio: The preeminent Roman orator, philosopher, and statesman. His vast corpus of speeches, letters, and philosophical works shaped Latin prose and Western thought. Works include Tusculan Disputations, On the Orator, and many famous orations.
author_notes: Life, career, and works are exceptionally well-documented.
representative_titles: Tusculanae Disputationes | Orator | De Lege Agraria | In Catilinam | Pro Murena | Pro Milone | Pro Q. Roscio Comoedo | Laelius de Amicitia

author_bio: Minor Latin author known only from a fragment on the Lupercalia festival.
author_notes: Only a single fragment survives; date and provenance are unknown.

author_bio: Marcus Valerius Martialis, known as Martial, was a Roman poet from Hispania, famous for his twelve books of Epigrams, which provide vivid insight into Roman society under the Flavian dynasty.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure.

author_bio: Roman mimographer known only from a single fragment of mime.
author_notes: Name and text from indirect tradition; uncertain identification.

author_bio: Roman matron, mother of Tiberius and Gaius Gracchus, known for her letters.
author_notes: Fragments of her letters survive; authenticity sometimes questioned.

author_bio: Roman poet of the neoteric school, known from fragments.
author_notes: Fragments of his poems survive.

author_bio: Maurus Servius Honoratus was a Late Antique grammarian and commentator, best known for his extensive commentaries on Virgil's works. His In Vergilii Aeneidos Libros and other grammatical treatises were central to medieval education and survive in multiple manuscripts.
author_notes: Well-attested historical author with a large surviving corpus; major source for Virgil scholia.
representative_titles: In Vergilii Aeneidos Libros | In Vergilii Bucolicon Librum | Commentarius in Artem Donati | In Vergilii Georgicon Libros | De Centum Metris | De Finalibus | De Metris Horatianis

author_bio: Roman politician and orator, known for his prosecution of Marcus Aemilius Scaurus. Only a few fragments of his speeches survive.
author_notes: Fragments preserved by Cicero and others.

author_bio: Roman politician and orator, tribune in 90 BCE. Fragments of his speeches and a few poetic lines survive.
author_notes: Attested in Cicero; fragmentary remains.

author_bio: A collection of anonymous mime poems from uncertain Latin poets, preserved in fragmentary form.
author_notes: The corpus of mime fragments attributed to uncertain poets.

author_bio: A Roman jurist and pontifex maximus, known for his legal writings. Only a single fragment of his work survives, cited by later legal sources.
author_notes: The fragment is known through citation; the corpus is extremely small.

author_bio: A Roman jurist and pontifex maximus, also known for some carmina (poems). Only a tiny fragment survives. He was a prominent legal authority and was murdered in 82 BCE.
author_notes: The poetic fragment is extremely brief; attribution is based on ancient references.

author_bio: A prominent Roman jurist and pontifex maximus, foundational figure in Roman law. Only fragments of his legal writings survive.
author_notes: Well-documented in legal tradition; works survive only in quotations.

author_bio: Author of Atellan farces, a genre of Italian comic drama. Possibly a Neronian or earlier writer, but identity is obscure.
author_notes: Very little is known; fragments suggest a comic playwright.

author_bio: A pioneering Roman poet and playwright, Naevius wrote the Bellum Punicum, a foundational epic, along with comedies (palliatae) and tragedies. He introduced the praetexta (historical drama) and is celebrated for his bold political satire.
author_notes: Fragments survive from multiple works; biography known from ancient sources, though some details are legendary.
representative_titles: alia carmina epica | Bellum Punicum | palliatae | tragoediae | carmina, frr. a Morel omissa | versus in Metellos sp. | praetextae

author_bio: Gnaeus Naevius was a Roman epic poet and dramatist, known for his Bellum Punicum and comedies.
author_notes: Historical figure; works survive in fragments.

author_bio: L. Neratius Priscus was a prominent Roman jurist of the early Empire, a member of the Proculian school, and a close associate of Emperor Trajan. His legal writings survive in fragmentary form within later compilations.
author_notes: Well-attested jurist; fragments preserved in the Digesta and Vatican Fragments.

author_bio: Nigidius Figulus was a Roman praetor and scholar, known for his grammatical and antiquarian works, much admired by Cicero.
author_notes: Fragmentary corpus; important for Roman intellectual history.

author_bio: Roman poet, said to have translated Homer's Iliad into Latin verse; only a single line survives.
author_notes: The surviving fragment is quoted by Priscian.

author_bio: Roman orator known only from a single fragment of a speech. His work survives in minute fragments cited by later grammarians.
author_notes: The only witness is a fragment preserved by Nonius Marcellus.

author_bio: Roman playwright of Atellan farces, known from fragments. His works are crude rustic comedies.
author_notes: Fragments preserved by Nonius and other grammarians.

author_bio: Numitorius was a Roman poet known only from a single fragment of his work Antibucolica, a response to Virgil's Eclogues.
author_notes: Extremely fragmentary; known only from a single quotation.

author_bio: Roman writer associated with agricultural and biographical topics, often identified as a contemporary of Caesar. Works survive only in fragments cited by later authors.
author_notes: Fragments of his works are quoted by Pliny the Elder and others.

author_bio: L. Orbilius Pupillus was a Roman grammarian known for his strict teaching methods and his influence on literary education. He is remembered primarily through Horace's epistle (Epist. 2.1.70–71) and fragments of his grammatical work.
author_notes: Attested as a historical grammarian; fragments of his grammatical work survive.

author_bio: Ovid (Publius Ovidius Naso) was a Roman poet, best known for his epic 'Metamorphoses', his love poetry in 'Amores' and 'Ars Amatoria', and his exile poetry 'Tristia' and 'Epistulae ex Ponto'. He was exiled by Augustus to Tomis on the Black Sea, where he died.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; extensive biographical and textual tradition.
representative_titles: Metamorphoses | Amores | Ars Amatoria | Fasti | Epistulae (vel Heroides) | Tristia | Epistulae ex Ponto | Remedia Amoris

author_bio: Sextus Paconianus was a Roman poet known for a single carmen preserved in fragmentary form. His work likely belongs to the early Imperial period, perhaps in the tradition of satirical or light verse.
author_notes: Extremely sparse remains; date and genre are conjectural.

author_bio: M. Pacuvius (c. 220–130 BCE) was a Roman tragic poet, known for his tragedies and praetextae; fragments of his works survive.
author_notes: Pacuvius is a major early Roman playwright.

author_bio: Papinius is a Latin poet known only from a single epigrammatic fragment preserved in the Latin Anthology. His identity is uncertain; the name may be a confusion with Statius's cognomen but the fragment suggests a separate minor poet.
author_notes: Very fragmentary evidence; the name and single fragment are all that survive.

author_bio: A major Latin poet of the Flavian period, author of the epic 'Thebaid', the unfinished 'Achilleid', and the occasional poems of the 'Silvae'. His work bridges Silver Latin epic and Hellenistic tradition.
author_notes: Biographical details are known from his poems and Martial.
representative_titles: Silvae | Achilleis | Thebais | De Bello Germanico (fragment)

author_bio: C. Papirius Carbo was a Roman orator and statesman of the late 2nd century BCE; one fragment of a speech survives.
author_notes: Only a fragment of one oration.

author_bio: A Roman orator and tribune, known only from a tiny speech fragment.
author_notes: Very sparse surviving evidence; 30 words only.

author_bio: A Christian priest and poet, known only from a few fragments of verse.
author_notes: Little known; possibly a late antique figure.

author_bio: Passienus Crispus was a Roman senator and orator, known for a single surviving oration fragment. He was a prominent figure under Claudius and noted for his wit and rhetorical skill.
author_notes: Minimal survival; cited by later authors.

author_bio: Paulus Quaestor was a Latin poet, known from a few surviving fragments of his carmina. The title 'Quaestor' suggests he held a public office, but his life remains obscure.
author_notes: Survives only in fragments; dates and biography are uncertain.

author_bio: Roman satirist and Stoic philosopher, author of six satires that critique Roman society with moral severity. His work was highly influential in antiquity and later.
author_notes: Complete satires survive; a standard author in Latin curricula.

author_bio: Petronius Arbiter, likely the same as Gaius Petronius, was a Roman courtier and author of the 'Satyrica,' a comic, picaresque novel that offers vivid, satirical glimpses of Neronian society. His work is a key text of Roman fiction.
author_notes: Identity is accepted by most scholars; fragments of the Satyrica survive.

author_bio: Roman fabulist, author of verse fables in the Aesopic tradition.
author_notes: Well-attested author.

author_bio: Philumenus was a Greek medical writer whose work on veterinary and human medicine survives in a Latin translation. Little is known of his life.
author_notes: Author known only from Latin translation of his work De medicina.

author_bio: A Roman grammarian and lexicographer, who compiled 'De Verborum Significatione', an abridgment of Verrius Flaccus's work on Latin word meanings, a key source for early Latin language and culture.
author_notes: Survives in fragmentary form; his name and work are well-attested.

author_display_name: Q. Pompeius Q. f. A. n. Rufus
author_bio: Roman politician and orator, possibly the son of the consul of 88 BCE. A single fragment of a speech survives.
author_notes: Very fragmentary; exact identification uncertain.

author_display_name: Q. Pompeius Q. f. Q. n. Rufus
author_bio: Roman orator known for a single surviving oration; his political career placed him in the turbulent late Republic.
author_notes: Filiation identifies him as Quintus Pompeius Rufus, grandson of Sulla. Very few textual remains.

author_bio: Roman historian of Gallic origin, known for his 'Historiae Philippicae', a universal history focused on the Hellenistic kingdoms, now lost but epitomized by Justin.
author_notes: Historical author; his original work is lost but known through the epitome of Justin.

author_bio: Pompilius is a shadowy figure; only a few lines of epigram and tragedy survive under his name.
author_notes: Attribution questionable.

author_bio: Roman jurist active in the Hadrianic period, known for a fragment of his Liber Regularum preserved in the Digest.
author_notes: The attribution of the fragment rests on legal sources; his career is otherwise poorly documented.

author_bio: A Roman writer of Atellan farces (Atellanae), known from fragments.
author_notes: Fragments attributed to him.

author_bio: Roman geographer, author of 'De Chorographia', the earliest surviving Latin work on geography.
author_notes: Complete work survives; well-attested.

author_bio: A late-antique grammarian and commentator, best known for his extensive scholia on Horace's works, covering poems, satires, epistles, and the Ars Poetica.
author_notes: Well-attested commentary tradition; dates approximate.
representative_titles: Commentum in Horati Carmina | Commentum in Horati Sermones | Commentum in Horati Epistulas | Commentum in Horati Epodos | Comment. in Hor. Artem Poet. | Vita Horati | Comment. in Hor. Carm. Saec.

author_bio: Publius Pomponius Secundus was a Roman tragic poet of the early Imperial period, known for composing both fabulae tragoediae and fabulae praetextae (historical dramas). Only fragments of his work survive, but he was highly regarded by contemporaries such as Quintilian.
author_notes: Fragmentary; his reputation is attested by ancient critics.

author_bio: Roman statesman, soldier, and writer; known for his strict moral conservatism and prolific authorship. Works include the agricultural manual 'De Agri Cultura' and the historical 'Origines'.
author_notes: Cato the Elder is a foundational figure in Latin literature.
representative_titles: Origines | De Agri Cultura | orationes | De Medicina | incertorum librorum fragmenta | De Re Militari | Carmen De Moribus | iurisprudentia

author_bio: M. Porcius Cato Uticensis (95–46 BCE) was a Roman statesman and Stoic philosopher, who also wrote orations, fragments of which survive.
author_notes: Only oration fragments survive.

author_bio: Early Roman poet and literary historian, author of a poem on literary chronology, only fragments of which survive.
author_notes: Known from quotations by later grammarians; his work is of interest for early Roman literary history.

author_display_name: M. Porcius M. f. M. n. Cato
author_bio: Marcus Porcius Cato, known as Cato the Elder or Cato the Censor, was a Roman statesman, orator, and writer, famous for his conservative values and his work De Agri Cultura.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; the filiation indicates his father and grandfather.

author_bio: A. Postumius Albinus was a Roman senator and historian, author of a history of Rome written in Greek. His work, known as Annales, survives only in a few fragments cited by later writers.
author_notes: Historical figure; fragments of his historical work survive.

author_bio: The 'Precatio Omnium Herbarum' is a short Latin poem, a prayer to all herbs, likely used in a medical or magical context. It is an anonymous work, and the title itself serves as the author heading in modern catalogs.
author_notes: The heading is the title of the work; no author is known.

author_bio: 'Precatio Terrae' (Prayer to the Earth) is a short Latin poem in the form of a prayer to the goddess Earth (Tellus), likely from the late Roman period. It is transmitted among the so-called 'poemata' of the Latin anthology.
author_notes: The poem is transmitted anonymously; this entry uses the title as author label.

author_bio: A collection of Latin poems dedicated to the god Priapus, often of an obscene nature, composed by various poets.
author_notes: The corpus is a compilation; individual authorship uncertain.

author_bio: A collection of Sententiae falsely attributed to the Roman scholar Varro, likely compiled in the Imperial period.
author_notes: The designation 'Pseudo-Varro' indicates a pseudepigraphical work.

author_bio: Publilius Optatianus Porfyrius was a Latin poet known for his highly stylized pattern poems (carmina figurata) and his epigrams. He was exiled and later restored to favor under Constantine, whose patronage he sought through his ingenious verses.
author_notes: Unique surviving corpus of figured poems; important for late antique literary culture.

author_bio: Roman writer of mimes and sententiae, best known for his collection of moral maxims that were widely quoted in antiquity.
author_notes: His sententiae were compiled from his mimes; the textual tradition is complex but his influence is clear.

author_bio: Possible Latin author known only from a verse fragment attributed to a 'Pupius'. The attribution is doubtful.
author_notes: The question mark indicates uncertain attribution; the fragment may be misattributed.

author_bio: Roman orator known solely from a fragment of a speech.
author_notes: Single fragment; otherwise unattested.

author_bio: A Roman comic playwright of togatae, known for his realistic dialogues. Fragments of his works survive, and an epigram also exists; he died young according to Jerome.
author_notes: His name is cited by ancient grammarians. The epigram attribution may be uncertain.

author_bio: Roman rhetorician and educator, author of the Institutio Oratoria, a comprehensive guide to rhetoric.
author_notes: Well-attested author.
representative_titles: Institutio Oratoria | Declamationes Minores | Declamationes Maiores sp.

author_bio: Quintus Curtius Rufus is the Roman historian who wrote a biography of Alexander the Great in Latin; his work survives largely intact.
author_notes: Only surviving Latin Alexander history.

author_bio: Quintus Tullius Cicero, younger brother of the orator Marcus Tullius Cicero, was a Roman politician and writer. He is credited with the 'Commentariolum Petitionis' (a handbook on electioneering) and composed poetic fragments.
author_notes: Attribution of the Commentariolum is sometimes questioned, but generally accepted.

author_bio: A Roman poet, possibly of the Augustan period, known from fragments.
author_notes: Uncertain identification and dates.

author_bio: Q. Remmius Palaemon was a Roman grammarian and teacher, known for his innovative grammatical handbook (Ars) that influenced later Latin grammarians. He was also a freedman and a controversial figure in literary circles.
author_notes: Attested historical grammarian; his Ars survives partly and is transmitted under his name, with some spuria.

author_bio: A Latin rhetorician, known for his treatise on rhetorical figures, 'Schemata Lexeos', a Latin adaptation of a Greek work by Gorgias.
author_notes: Work survives incomplete; biographical details are sparse.

author_bio: Roman politician and historian, wrote an autobiography.
author_notes: Only a fragment of his autobiography survives.

author_bio: Shadowy figure known only from a grammatical fragment. Possibly a minor grammarian or scholiast of uncertain date.
author_notes: Only a single short grammatical fragment attests this author.

author_bio: A ritual text listing the Roman Argei shrines, an important document of early Roman religious topography.
author_notes: This is a textual record of a ritual, not an author. The title occupies the author slot.

author_bio: A translation of biblical books into the Sahidic dialect of Coptic, widely used in early Egyptian Christianity. The corpus includes several New Testament books. The label here is used as a work title for the version.
author_notes: Filed as a work title because 'Sahiddic Coptic Bible' is a text corpus, not a person or collective author.
representative_titles: James | 1 Thessalonians | 2 Thessalonians | Romans | Luke | 1 Timothy | The Acts | Galatians

author_bio: Sallust (Gaius Sallustius Crispus) was a Roman historian and politician, known for his monographs on the Catilinarian conspiracy and the Jugurthine War, as well as a larger history (Historiae). His works are characterized by a vivid style and moralizing tone and are a key source for late Republican history.
author_notes: Well-attested historical author with substantial surviving works; minor spuria included.
representative_titles: Bellum Iugurthinum | Catilinae Coniuratio | Historiae | Historiarum frr. e papyris | Ad Caesarem de Re Publ. sp. | Historiarum frr. e codicibus | In M. Tullium Ciceronem sp. | Historiarum frr. ampliora

author_bio: Obscure Roman author of grammatical and tragic works, known only from fragments.
author_notes: Very few fragments.

author_bio: Saserna was a Roman writer on agriculture, known for his work 'De Agri Cultura'. He is cited by later agricultural authors like Columella, but his life is otherwise unknown.
author_notes: Survives only in fragments; identity and dates are inferred from citations.

author_bio: A Roman orator known only from a fragment of a speech.
