author_notes: Very little known; identity and dates uncertain.

author_bio: Roman tragic poet of the early Empire; a single line of his work survives.
author_notes: Extremely fragmentary; barely attested.

author_bio: Roman senator and orator, father of the tribune Curio. A fragment of a speech survives.
author_notes: The designation 'avus' distinguishes him from his son.

author_bio: Gaius Scribonius Curio, the elder, was a Roman orator and politician, known for his speeches.
author_notes: Historical figure; only fragments of his oratory survive.

author_bio: Roman physician, author of a pharmacological compendium.
author_notes: Well-attested author.

author_bio: A group of six (or more) purported authors of the Historia Augusta, a collection of imperial biographies from Hadrian to Carinus. Their identity is contested, and the work is likely a product of a single late antique author or group.
author_notes: The collective authorship is conventional; the work is considered a single composition by most scholars.
representative_titles: Commodus Antoninus Aeli Lampridi | Iuli Capitolini Verus | Gordiani Tres Iuli Capitolini | Vita Marci Antonini Philosophi Iuli Capitolini | Maximini Duo Iuli Capitolini | Aeli Spartiani Severus | Trebelli Pollionis Divus Claudius | Helvius Pertinax Iuli Capitolini

author_bio: Sempronius Asellio was a Roman historian, known for his work 'Rerum Gestarum Libri'. He is cited by later authors for his historical writings, but his life is poorly documented.
author_notes: Survives only in fragments; dates and biography are inferred from citations.

author_bio: Roman reformer and orator, tribune of the plebs in 123–122 BCE. Fragments of his speeches survive.
author_notes: Historical figure; his orations were influential in the late Republic.

author_bio: Roman rhetorician and writer, father of Seneca the Younger; known for his collections of controversiae and suasoriae that preserve the practice of declamation.
author_notes: Often called Seneca the Elder or Seneca Rhetor; his work survives in excerpted manuscripts.

author_bio: Roman poet known only from a short fragment.
author_notes: Only 44 words survive.

author_bio: A Roman poet known from a fragmentary carmen.
author_notes: Little known; dates uncertain.

author_bio: The Greek translation of the Hebrew Bible, traditionally attributed to seventy-two Jewish scholars in Alexandria.
author_notes: Used as a work title; collective translation project, not individual authorship.
representative_titles: Genesis | Psalms | Ezekiel | Jeremiah | Deuteronomy | Lamentations | Malachi | Isaiah

author_bio: A Roman poet and medical writer, author of the Liber Medicinalis, a didactic poem on remedies. He may be the same as Serenus Sammonicus, a scholar at the court of Severus Alexander.
author_notes: The name appears as 'Sammonicus' in some manuscripts; the attribution is standard, though identity with the elder Sammonicus is debated.

author_bio: Roman politician and orator, possibly the consul of 106 BCE or his son. Fragments of a speech survive.
author_notes: Uncertain identity; possibly the consul of 106 BCE.

author_bio: Roman politician and orator, a populares ally of Saturninus; his oratory is preserved in a single fragment.
author_notes: The input has 'C. Servilius Glaucia' matching the historical figure; the id suggests orations. Corrected spelling: Glaucia (not Rufus).

author_bio: Obscure Roman poet or grammarian known only from fragments.
author_notes: Only a single fragment survives.

author_bio: Sextilius Ena was a Latin poet active during the Augustan period, known from a single citation by Seneca the Elder. His work, likely an epic or panegyric poem, survives only as a fragment of six words.
author_notes: Very fragmentary evidence; known only from a citation in Seneca's Suasoriae.

author_bio: Sextus Propertius (c. 50–15 BCE) was a Roman elegiac poet, known for his four books of Elegiae.
author_notes: Large surviving corpus of elegies.

author_bio: A Roman writer on land surveying (gromatici), author of De Condicionibus Agrorum, a technical manual on land classification and boundary disputes. His work was used by later surveyors.
author_notes: His name suggests Sicilian origin, but evidence is slim; the work survives in the Corpus Agrimensorum Romanorum.

author_bio: Sinnius Capito was a Roman grammarian and antiquarian of the late Republic, known for works on Latin language, orthography, and religious law. Fragments of his grammatical writings survive in later compilers such as Festus and Charisius.
author_notes: Fragmentary remains; his works are cited by later grammarians.

author_bio: A Roman grammarian and freedman, known for a single fragment of grammatical writing. He was a teacher in Rome and is mentioned by Suetonius as a notable scholar.
author_notes: The fragment is tiny; his biographical information comes from Suetonius (De Grammaticis).

author_bio: Sueius is a minor Roman poet of the Augustan period, known from a few surviving poetic fragments. His identity is very obscure, and he is sometimes identified with a friend of Ovid.
author_notes: Minimal survival; doubtful identification.

author_bio: Gaius Suetonius Tranquillus was a Roman historian and biographer, best known for his Lives of the Caesars, a key source for the early Roman Empire.
author_notes: Historical figure with multiple surviving works.
representative_titles: De Vita Caesarum | De Grammaticis et Rhetoribus | De Poetis | Prata | De Historicis | fragmenta

author_bio: Servius Sulpicius Rufus was a Roman jurist and orator, a student of Cicero and a leading legal authority of his time.
author_notes: Well-documented figure, though only fragments of his legal writings survive.

author_bio: The Tabulae Censoriae were official records of the Roman censors; only fragments quoting them survive.
author_notes: Quotation fragments from an official document.

author_bio: Tarquitius Priscus was a Roman author of Etruscan origin who wrote on Etruscan discipline ('De Disciplina Etrusca'). Only fragments survive, reflecting the syncretism of Etruscan and Roman religious practices.
author_notes: Known only through citations in later Latin authors.

author_bio: Terence was a Roman playwright of African origin, author of six comedies that survive complete, known for refined language and plot construction.
author_notes: One of the most important Roman comic poets.
representative_titles: Andria | Heauton Timorumenos | Eunuchus | Phormio | Adelphoe | Hecyra

author_bio: A Latin grammarian from Mauretania, author of a metric treatise in verse (De litteris, syllabis, metris).
author_notes: Text transmitted in full; important for late antique metrics.
representative_titles: De Litt., De Syll., De Metr.

author_bio: Roman grammarian, author of works on orthography and grammar.
author_notes: Fragments of his works survive.
representative_titles: De Orthographia | De Adverbio et Praeposit. | De ordinat. part. orat. sp. | fr. in codice Parisino 7520

author_bio: An encyclopedic Roman scholar and prolific author, Varro wrote on language, agriculture, religion, and history. His surviving works include De Lingua Latina and Res Rusticae, while his Menippean satires survive in fragments. He was a key figure in the intellectual life of the late Republic.
author_notes: Extensive biographical and textual tradition; many works are lost, but the surviving corpus is large.
representative_titles: De Lingua Latina | Res Rusticae | Antiquitates Rerum Humanarum | Antiquitates Rerum Divinarum | fragmenta grammatica | Menippeae | Logistorici | Annales

author_bio: A Roman poet of the late Republic, known for didactic and epic poetry; a younger contemporary of Catullus and Lucretius.
author_notes: Fragments survive; limited to high-specialist readership.

author_bio: Pliny the Elder (Gaius Plinius Secundus) was a Roman author, naturalist, and naval commander. He is best known for his encyclopedic work 'Naturalis Historia', a vast compendium of natural knowledge, and also wrote on grammar in 'Dubius Sermo'. He died in the eruption of Mount Vesuvius.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; extensive biographical information available.

author_bio: Roman author and administrator, known for his 'Epistulae', a collection of letters offering insight into Roman public and private life, and his 'Panegyricus' on Trajan.
author_notes: Well-attested historical author; his correspondence is a major source for the period.

author_bio: Silius Italicus was a Roman senator and epic poet, author of the Punica, a historical epic on the Second Punic War.
author_notes: Well-known Flavian poet.

author_bio: Roman elegiac poet renowned for his refined and melancholic love poetry, central to the Augustan elegiac tradition.
author_notes: Major Augustan poet; the Tibullan corpus includes poems of disputed authorship (corpus Tibullianum).

author_bio: A Roman poet of the late Republic, author of erotic verse; mentioned by Ovid and others.
author_notes: Fragments only; known from citations and literary references.

author_bio: A Roman playwright of fabulae togatae (comedy in Italian settings). Titinius was one of the earliest Latin comic dramatists; only fragments survive, noted for their colloquial style.
author_notes: Known only from citations by later grammarians; his plays are not preserved intact.

author_bio: Roman orator, known from a fragment of a speech.
author_notes: Only a fragment of his oratory survives.

author_bio: Barely known grammarian, attested by a single fragment. Possibly a common Roman name insufficient for secure identification.
author_notes: Only a few words survive; identity uncertain.

author_period: late 1st century BCE – early 1st century CE
author_bio: Preeminent Roman historian whose monumental work 'Ab Urbe Condita' covered the history of Rome from its foundation to the reign of Augustus. An iconic figure in Latin historiography.
author_notes: Well-attested historical author. His history originally consisted of 142 books, of which 35 survive. The Periochae are summaries of lost books.
representative_titles: Ab Urbe Condita | fragmenta | Periochae Librorum A. U. C. | A.U.C. Perioch. ex P.Oxy.668

author_bio: Roman comic playwright, author of twenty-one surviving comedies that form the core of Latin comic drama. Plautus adapted Greek New Comedy for Roman audiences with lively dialogue and song.
author_notes: A canonically central figure in Latin literature; many plays survive complete.
representative_titles: Bacchides | Menaechmi | Amphitruo | Mercator | Asinaria | Curculio | Mostellaria | Pseudolus

author_cultural_context: Latin literature, early Roman drama (fabulae palliatae)
author_bio: Trabea was a Roman comic playwright of the late 2nd to early 1st century BCE, known chiefly through fragmentary remains attributed to the genre of fabulae palliatae. Only a single surviving work is recorded, providing scant evidence for his style and themes.
author_notes: Extremely fragmentary transmission; biographical details are unknown and the attribution relies on ancient testimonia.

author_bio: Roman jurist and friend of Cicero, known for legal writings. Trebatius was a prominent legal authority whose works survive only in fragments.
author_notes: Fragments of his legal works are preserved in later compilations.

author_bio: Gnaeus Tremelius Scrofa was a Roman writer on agriculture, known for his work 'De Re Rustica'. He is cited by later agricultural authors like Varro and Columella, but his life is otherwise unknown.
author_notes: Survives only in fragments; dates and biography are uncertain.

author_bio: A freedman of Cicero, known for an epigram on Cicero's death.
author_notes: Fragmentary attribution.

author_bio: Tiro was a freedman and secretary of Cicero, inventor of Tironian notes, and author of grammatical works and a biography of Cicero.
author_notes: Fragmentary; known through references by later authors.

author_bio: Turnus was a Roman satirist of the early Imperial period, known for a satura preserved only in a minuscule fragment. He may be the same Turnus mentioned by Martial and Pliny as a poet of some repute.
author_notes: Fragmentary survival; identity conflated with a mythological figure but here treated as a historical person.

author_bio: Roman comic playwright (palliata), known from substantial fragments of several plays. His work shows influence of Greek New Comedy.
author_notes: Fragments in Ribbeck, CRF.

author_bio: Author of a lost work on agriculture, known only from a brief mention.
author_notes: Very fragmentary; uncertain dates.

author_bio: Vagellius was a Latin poet, known only from a single surviving fragment of a poem (carmen). His life and works are otherwise unattested.
author_notes: Known only from a brief fragment; biographical details are entirely unknown.

author_bio: Roman poet known only from a single quotation by Varro; nothing else is known of his life or work.
author_notes: The name alone provides no secure identification; possibly a fragment from an otherwise unknown author.

author_bio: Roman poet of the late Republic, known for a few surviving epigrams that show Hellenistic influence.
author_notes: Associated with the so-called 'neoteric' pre-Catullan epigrammatists.

author_bio: A Roman annalist whose historical work, the Annales, survives only in fragments; used as a source by Livy.
author_notes: Historical figure but work fragmentary.

author_bio: Author of the 'Facta et Dicta Memorabilia', a collection of historical anecdotes used for rhetorical training.
author_notes: Well-attested; widely transmitted.

author_bio: M. Valerius Messalla Corvinus was a Roman general, politician, orator, and literary patron. He served as consul and was a supporter of Augustus. His orations and historical commentaries survive only in fragments, but he is remembered as a key figure in the Augustan literary circle.
author_notes: Well-documented historical figure; his writings are fragmentary but cited by later authors.

author_bio: Roman aristocrat and scholar known for antiquarian works on Roman augury and families. He wrote De auspiciis and De familiis Romanis, fragments of which survive.
author_notes: Cited as a source on Roman religious and genealogical lore.

author_bio: Roman grammarian and textual critic known for his work on Latin authors, especially his annotated editions and his 'De Notis Iuris'.
author_notes: Historical figure: his work as a scholar is well attested.
representative_titles: Vita Persii | De Notis Iuris | fragmenta

author_cultural_context: Latin poetry, didactic and satirical verse (carmina)
author_bio: Quintus Valerius Soranus was a Roman poet and grammarian of the late 2nd to early 1st century BCE. He wrote didactic carmina on mythological and geographical themes, but only fragments survive. He is noted for his learning and is sometimes cited by later authors.
author_notes: Fragmentary survives; known through testimonia and citations.

author_bio: C. Valgius Rufus was a Roman poet and literary figure of the Augustan age; only fragments of his verse survive.
author_notes: Mentioned by Horace and others.

author_bio: L. Varius Rufus was a Roman poet of the Augustan period, praised by his contemporaries; only fragments of his poems and tragedies survive.
author_notes: Mentioned by Horace and Virgil.

author_bio: Velius Longus was a Roman grammarian of the 2nd century CE, known for a treatise De Orthographia (On Orthography). This work, preserved in substantial form, discusses Latin spelling and sound-change, with numerous examples from early Latin authors.
author_notes: Relatively well-preserved grammatical work; biographical details are sparse.

author_bio: Roman historian and officer, author of the 'Historia Romana', a concise history of Rome from its origins to the reign of Tiberius.
author_notes: His work survives almost complete, though with a lacuna.

author_bio: Veranius was a Roman antiquarian writer, known for his work on sacred matters (libri de rebus sacris). He is cited by later authors for his contributions to Roman religious law and antiquities.
author_notes: Survives only in fragments; identity and dates are inferred from citations.

author_bio: The preeminent Roman poet, author of the epic 'Aeneid', the didactic 'Georgics', and the pastoral 'Eclogues'. His work shaped Western literature.
author_notes: Widely studied and transmitted in numerous manuscripts.

author_bio: Roman senator and general; his only literary remains are a couple of epigrammatic lines.
author_notes: Epigram known via Pliny; identity as author of those lines is clear.

author_bio: Marcus Verrius Flaccus was a Roman grammarian and teacher, known for his works on grammar and Roman antiquities.
author_notes: Works survive only in fragments and later summaries.

author_bio: Q. Vibius Crispus was a Roman senator and orator, known for his sharp wit and rhetorical skill. He survived under multiple emperors and was a noted delator. His speeches are lost except for a few fragments cited by Tacitus and others.
author_notes: Historical figure; his orations are fragmentary but he is mentioned by Tacitus and Quintilian.

author_bio: The 'Vita Iuvenalis' is a short anonymous biography of the satirist Juvenal, transmitted in manuscripts of his works. It provides traditional details about Juvenal's life, education, and exile.
author_notes: The title of the biography serves as the author label; the writer is unknown.

author_bio: Marcus Vitruvius Pollio was a Roman architect and engineer, best known for his comprehensive work De Architectura, which influenced Renaissance architecture.
author_notes: Historical figure with a fully surviving work.

author_bio: Volcacius Sedigitus was a Roman poet of the 1st century BCE, known for a didactic or satirical poem on metric and literary matters. His single surviving carmen is fragmentary but attests to his interest in poetic theory.
author_notes: Fragmentary preservation; name and work appear in later grammatical sources.

author_bio: Volumnius was a Roman poet known only from a single fragment of a poem.
author_notes: Extremely fragmentary; nothing else known.

author_bio: Roman jurist and public official, author of a treatise on the distribution of the as (monetary system).
author_notes: Fragmentary legal/economic text; known from later compilations.

author_bio: Zeno was bishop of Verona in the mid-4th century. His Tractatus are a collection of sermons blending biblical exegesis with moral exhortation, reflecting North Italian patristic theology.
author_notes: Well-attested bishop; his works survive in a single manuscript but have been edited critically.
